title: Project: Build the Community Website History
summary: Create a readable sequence of cohesive commits for a small community website instead of saving unrelated changes in one snapshot.

sketchBlocks.title: Building a Cohesive Commit History
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
In this project, you'll work as a junior web maintainer for a community center website. The goal is to create a readable commit history that clearly documents changes to the website's home page and events page. This will help other developers understand the updates without needing to open every file. You'll learn to use Git commands to inspect changes, stage them selectively, and commit them with meaningful messages. By the end of this project, you'll have a clean, organized commit history that reflects the website's development accurately.

### Project Brief



**Scenario:** A community center website has a plain home page and an events page. Staff want the next updates recorded as a history that another developer can understand without opening every file.

**Your role:** Junior web maintainer organizing a readable local history

**Workspace:** One cumulative terminal workspace rooted at community-site/ with index.html, events.html, styles.css, and an initialized repository

**Deliverable:** A six-step history with separate navigation and events commits, exact authored messages, preserved file content, and a clean final working tree.

sketchBlocks.title: Using Git Diff to Inspect Changes
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: Imagine you've made some changes to the community website's navigation bar in `index.html`. Before committing, it's crucial to inspect these changes to ensure they are correct and complete. Use `git status` to see which files have been modified. Then, use `git diff` to view the specific changes in `index.html`. This command shows line-by-line differences between the working directory and the last commit, helping you verify the changes before staging them.

sketchBlocks.title: Selective Staging with Git Add
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: After inspecting the changes in `index.html`, you decide to stage only the navigation updates. Use `git add index.html` to stage these changes. It's important to stage only related changes to keep commits focused. You can verify what's staged by using `git diff --staged`, which shows the differences between the staged files and the last commit. This ensures that only the intended changes are included in the next commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Creating Meaningful Commits
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: With the navigation changes staged, it's time to commit them. Use `git commit -m "Add primary navigation"` to create a commit with a clear, descriptive message. This message should convey the purpose of the changes, making it easier for others to understand the commit's intent. A well-written commit message is crucial for maintaining a readable project history.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspecting Commit History with Git Log
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: After making several commits, you can review the project's history using `git log --oneline`. This command provides a concise view of the commit history, showing commit hashes and messages. To inspect a specific commit in detail, use `git show <commit-hash>`. This reveals the changes made in that commit, helping you understand the project's evolution over time.

quizDraft.title: Inspect the First Website Change
quizDraft.prompt:
Use `git status` and `git diff` to identify the changes made to `index.html` without staging them. Type the commands and press Enter to see the output.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter, then type each requested Git command and press Enter.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

starterFiles.content:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then complete the requested Git task.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
git init -q -b main
git config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '.zoeskoul/
main.sh
' >> .git/info/exclude
printf 'Git practice file 1\n' > index.html

quizDraft.title: Stage Only the Navigation Update
quizDraft.prompt:
Stage the changes in `index.html` for the navigation update. Use `git add` and verify with `git diff --staged`. Type the commands and press Enter.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter, then type each requested Git command and press Enter.
